Judas Iscariot.name: Judas Iscariot - The Betrayer
Judas Iscariot.story: Judas was one of the twelve who betrayed Jesus (Matthew 26-27, Mark 14, Luke 22, John 13, 18). He kept the money bag and stole from it. Satan entered him. He agreed to betray Jesus for thirty silver coins. He identified Jesus with a kiss. When he saw Jesus was condemned, he returned the money and hanged himself. The field was called the Field of Blood.
themes: Betrayal: He handed over his Master
Money: He valued silver over the Saviour
Remorse: He regretted but did not repent
Judas Iscariot.culturalImpact: Judas's name is synonymous with betrayal.

Mary Magdalene.name: Mary Magdalene - First Witness of the Resurrection
Mary Magdalene.story: Mary Magdalene was from Magdala (Luke 8, Matthew 27-28, Mark 15-16, John 19-20). Jesus had cast seven demons from her. She followed Him and supported His ministry. She stood at the cross. She went to the tomb on the first day of the week. She saw the stone rolled away and ran to tell the disciples. She wept outside the tomb. Jesus appeared to her first. He said her name; she recognised Him and proclaimed, "I have seen the Lord."
themes: Deliverance: Jesus freed her from demons
Loyalty: She followed to the cross and tomb
First Witness: She was the first to see the risen Jesus
Mary Magdalene.culturalImpact: Mary Magdalene is honoured as the apostle to the apostles.

Bartimaeus.name: Bartimaeus - The Blind Man Who Saw
Bartimaeus.story: Bartimaeus was a blind beggar near Jericho (Mark 10, Luke 18). When he heard Jesus was passing, he cried out for mercy. The crowd told him to be quiet. He cried out more. Jesus stopped and called him. He threw aside his cloak and came. Jesus asked what he wanted. He said to see. Jesus healed him, and he followed Jesus.
themes: Persistence: He cried out despite opposition
Faith: Jesus said his faith had healed him
Following: He followed Jesus on the road
Bartimaeus.culturalImpact: Bartimaeus is a model of persistent faith.

Caiaphas.name: Caiaphas - High Priest at Jesus' Trial
Caiaphas.story: Caiaphas was high priest when Jesus was arrested (Matthew 26, John 11, 18). He had said it was better for one man to die than for the whole nation to perish. He questioned Jesus and tore his clothes when Jesus said He was the Son of God. He sent Jesus to Pilate. He was involved in the persecution of the apostles.
themes: Political Calculation: He sought to preserve the nation
Rejection: He rejected Jesus as Messiah
Caiaphas.culturalImpact: Caiaphas represents the religious establishment that condemned Jesus.

Annas.name: Annas - High Priest
Annas.story: Annas was high priest when Jesus was arrested (John 18, Luke 3). He had been high priest and was the father-in-law of Caiaphas. Jesus was first brought to him. He questioned Jesus about His disciples and teaching. Jesus said He had spoken openly. Annas sent Him to Caiaphas.
themes: Authority: He was a powerful religious leader
Jesus' Trial: He was part of the process that led to the cross
Annas.culturalImpact: Annas represents the religious establishment that opposed Jesus.

Barabbas.name: Barabbas - The Prisoner Released
Barabbas.story: Barabbas was a prisoner who had committed murder in an insurrection (Matthew 27, Mark 15, Luke 23, John 18). At Passover, Pilate offered to release a prisoner. The crowd chose Barabbas over Jesus. Pilate released him and handed Jesus over to be crucified. Barabbas was spared while the innocent Jesus died.
themes: Substitution: Jesus died in his place
Choice: The crowd chose the wrong man
Barabbas.culturalImpact: Barabbas represents the guilty one who goes free while Christ bears the penalty.

Centurion at Capernaum.name: Centurion at Capernaum - Man of Great Faith
Centurion at Capernaum.story: The centurion at Capernaum had a servant who was paralysed (Matthew 8, Luke 7). He asked Jesus to heal him. He said he was not worthy to have Jesus under his roof but only to say the word. Jesus marvelled and said He had not found such faith in Israel. The servant was healed.
themes: Faith: He believed Jesus could heal from a distance
Humility: He did not consider himself worthy
Centurion at Capernaum.culturalImpact: The centurion is a model of faith for all generations.

Centurion at the Cross.name: Centurion at the Cross - Who Confessed Christ
Centurion at the Cross.story: The centurion at the cross oversaw Jesus' crucifixion (Matthew 27, Mark 15, Luke 23). When Jesus died and the earth shook, he said, "Surely this man was the Son of God" (or "a righteous man"). He had seen the manner of Jesus' death.
themes: Recognition: He acknowledged Jesus' identity
Witness: A Gentile confessed at the cross
Centurion at the Cross.culturalImpact: The centurion's confession is a pivotal moment in the passion narrative.

Cleopas.name: Cleopas - On the Road to Emmaus
Cleopas.story: Cleopas was one of two disciples walking to Emmaus (Luke 24). Jesus joined them but they did not recognise Him. He asked what they were discussing. They told of Jesus' death and the women's report of the empty tomb. Jesus explained the Scriptures. At the meal, He broke bread and they recognised Him. They returned to Jerusalem and told the eleven.
themes: Encounter: He met the risen Jesus
Recognition: He knew Him in the breaking of bread
Cleopas.culturalImpact: The road to Emmaus is one of the most beloved resurrection stories.

Peter.name: Peter - The Rock
Peter.story:
Peter was a fisherman from Galilee who became the leader of the apostles (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, Acts). His name was Simon; Jesus called him Cephas (Peter), meaning rock. He was bold and impulsive: he walked on water, confessed Jesus as the Christ, and drew his sword in Gethsemane. He also denied Jesus three times and wept bitterly.

After the resurrection, Jesus restored Peter and commissioned him to feed His sheep. At Pentecost, Peter preached and thousands believed. He led the early church, healed the lame man, and faced the Sanhedrin. He received the vision that led to Cornelius' conversion and the inclusion of the Gentiles. He wrote two epistles and was martyred in Rome.

Peter is the apostle of bold faith and costly restoration. He stumbled but was restored. He opened the door to Jews and Gentiles. He is the rock on which Christ built His church.
themes: Bold Faith: Peter stepped out of the boat and confessed Christ
Failure and Restoration: He denied Jesus but was restored and used mightily
Keys of the Kingdom: He opened the door to Jews at Pentecost and Gentiles with Cornelius
Shepherd: Jesus charged him to feed His sheep
Peter.culturalImpact: Peter is the most prominent apostle in the Gospels and Acts. He is honoured as the first pope in Catholic tradition. His epistles are Scripture. He represents both the strength and weakness of disciples, and the power of Christ's restoration.

Andrew.name: Andrew - The First Disciple
Andrew.story: Andrew was Peter's brother and one of the twelve (John 1, 6, 12). He was a disciple of John the Baptist and followed Jesus when John pointed Him out. He found his brother Peter and brought him to Jesus. He brought the boy with loaves and fishes to Jesus. With Philip, he brought Greeks who wanted to see Jesus.
themes: Bringing Others: He brought Peter and others to Jesus
Quiet Service: He worked behind the scenes
Andrew.culturalImpact: Andrew is the patron saint of bringing people to Christ.

James (the Lord's Brother).name: James - Brother of Jesus and Church Leader
James (the Lord's Brother).story: James was a brother of Jesus who did not believe during His ministry (Matthew 13, John 7, Acts 15, James). After the resurrection, Jesus appeared to him. He became a leader in the Jerusalem church. He presided at the council on circumcision. He wrote the epistle of James. He was known for his piety. He was martyred by being thrown from the temple.
themes: Conversion: He believed after the resurrection
Leadership: He led the Jerusalem church
Practical Faith: His letter emphasises works
James (the Lord's Brother).culturalImpact: James's epistle is a key New Testament letter.

Barnabas.name: Barnabas - Son of Encouragement
Barnabas.story: Barnabas was a Levite from Cyprus (Acts 4, 9, 11-15). He sold land and gave the money to the apostles. He brought Saul to the apostles when they were afraid. He was sent to Antioch and brought Saul there. They were sent on the first missionary journey. They disagreed over John Mark and parted. Barnabas took Mark to Cyprus.
themes: Encouragement: His name means son of encouragement
Partnership: He worked with Paul
Second Chances: He gave Mark another opportunity
Barnabas.culturalImpact: Barnabas is a model of encouragement and grace.

Timothy.name: Timothy - Paul's Son in the Faith
Timothy.story: Timothy was from Lystra, son of a Jewish mother and Greek father (Acts 16, 1-2 Timothy). Paul took him on his journeys. He was circumcised to minister to Jews. He was with Paul in many trials. Paul wrote two letters to him about pastoral ministry. He was young but faithful. Paul said he had no one like him.
themes: Discipleship: He learned from Paul
Youth: He led despite his age
Faithfulness: He was reliable and devoted
Timothy.culturalImpact: Timothy is a model for young leaders in the church.

Apollos.name: Apollos - Eloquent Teacher
Apollos.story: Apollos was a Jew from Alexandria, an eloquent man versed in Scripture (Acts 18, 1 Corinthians). He taught accurately about Jesus though he knew only John's baptism. Priscilla and Aquila explained the way more accurately. He greatly helped the believers in Achaia and vigorously refuted the Jews. Paul later referred to him as a fellow worker.
themes: Teaching: He was gifted and knowledgeable
Growth: He received further instruction
Partnership: He worked with Paul
Apollos.culturalImpact: Apollos represents learned, effective teachers in the church.

Cornelius.name: Cornelius - First Gentile Convert
Cornelius.story: Cornelius was a centurion in Caesarea, a devout God-fearer (Acts 10). An angel told him to send for Peter. Peter had a vision of unclean animals and heard "Do not call anything impure that God has made clean." Peter came and preached. The Holy Spirit fell on Cornelius and his household. They were baptised. This opened the door for the Gentiles.
themes: God-Fearer: He worshipped God though not a Jew
Breakthrough: His conversion marked the inclusion of the Gentiles
Cornelius.culturalImpact: Cornelius's conversion was a turning point for the church.

Esther.name: Esther - Queen Who Saved Her People
Esther.story:
Esther was a Jewish orphan who became queen of Persia and saved her people from genocide (Esther). Her Hebrew name was Hadassah. When King Ahasuerus sought a new queen, Esther was taken to the palace. Her cousin Mordecai advised her to hide her identity. She won the king's favour and became queen.

When Haman plotted to destroy the Jews, Mordecai urged Esther to approach the king. She risked her life, for anyone who approached unsummoned could be put to death. She said, "If I perish, I perish." She revealed Haman's plot and her own identity. The king had Haman executed and issued a decree allowing the Jews to defend themselves. The Jews were saved.

Esther's story shows God's providence working through human courage. She was "for such a time as this." She is honoured in Jewish tradition at Purim, when her deliverance is celebrated.
themes: For Such a Time: Esther was placed in position to save her people
Courage: She risked her life to approach the king
Providence: God worked through ordinary events to deliver His people
Identity: She embraced her Jewish identity when it mattered most
Esther.culturalImpact: Esther is celebrated at Purim. Her story has inspired courage in the face of persecution. She appears in art as a queen. The book of Esther is unique: God's name is not mentioned, yet His hand is evident throughout.

Ammon.name: Ammon - Nation East of Israel
Ammon.story: Ammon was the nation descended from Ben-Ammi, Lot's grandson (Genesis 19). The Ammonites lived east of the Jordan and often fought Israel. Jephthah defeated them. David defeated them and took the crown of their king. They opposed Nehemiah's rebuilding. God judged them for their hostility.
themes: Origin: Descended from Lot's daughter
Enmity: They opposed Israel repeatedly
Ammon.culturalImpact: Ammon appears throughout the Old Testament as Israel's neighbour and foe.

Aram (Syria) and Ben-hadad.name: Aram and Ben-hadad - Syrian Foes
Aram (Syria) and Ben-hadad.story: Aram (Syria) was Israel's northern neighbour. Ben-hadad was a common name for Syrian kings. They often warred with Israel. Ahab defeated Ben-hadad with God's help. Elisha had dealings with Syrian kings. Hazael, another Syrian king, oppressed Israel. The Syrians were a recurring threat.
themes: Conflict: Syria and Israel were often at war
Prophets: Elijah and Elisha dealt with Syrian kings
Aram (Syria) and Ben-hadad.culturalImpact: Aram appears throughout the historical books.

Assyria and Sennacherib.name: Assyria and Sennacherib - Empire and King
Assyria and Sennacherib.story: Assyria was the empire that conquered the northern kingdom of Israel. Sennacherib was its king who besieged Jerusalem in Hezekiah's day (2 Kings 18-19, Isaiah 36-37). He sent his commander to demand surrender. Hezekiah prayed. Isaiah prophesied that Sennacherib would not enter the city. The angel of the Lord struck the Assyrian camp, and Sennacherib returned home and was killed by his sons.
themes: Threat: Assyria was a mighty empire
Deliverance: God saved Jerusalem from Sennacherib
Assyria and Sennacherib.culturalImpact: Sennacherib's defeat is a famous example of divine deliverance.

Babylon and Nebuchadnezzar.name: Babylon and Nebuchadnezzar - Empire and King
Babylon and Nebuchadnezzar.story: Babylon was the empire that conquered Judah and destroyed Jerusalem. Nebuchadnezzar was its greatest king (2 Kings 24-25, Daniel). He besieged Jerusalem, took captives, and eventually destroyed the temple. He had dreams that Daniel interpreted. He made a golden image and threw the three Hebrews into the furnace. He was humbled and acknowledged the Most High.
themes: Conquest: Babylon destroyed the kingdom
Humility: Nebuchadnezzar was brought low and restored
Babylon and Nebuchadnezzar.culturalImpact: Babylon and Nebuchadnezzar are central to the exile narrative.
